text: The surgical team positions the patient in a dead lateral position for the adrenalectomy. A port is placed in the midclavicular line under the costal margin, two fingers below. The laparoscopic view reveals the spleen and surrounding abdominal structures.

The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure, lowering it to enter the space between the splenic flexure and the kidney. The dissection continues between the kidney, pancreas, and spleen, moving within the pancreatic-spanning plane. The spleen and pancreas are lifted to access the pancreatic lock.

The surgeon is in a lateral position, using gravity to assist in mobilizing the spinopancreatic block. The pancreas is visible, and the surgeon carefully manipulates the pancreatic tissue to approach the spleen, distal pancreas, suprarenal gland, and kidney. The golden yellow color of the suprarenal gland is observed, and gas is inserted to facilitate the process.

The surgeon continues to develop the surgical plane between the pancreas, suprarenal gland, and kidney, further separating the splenopancreatic block from the suprarenal gland. This maneuver aims to expose the inferior phrenic vessels, with the splenopancreatic block moving to the right side to reveal the targeted area.

The surgeon uses a ligature device to expand the space around the phrenic vessels, which are now clearly visible. The left adrenal vein is observed making a confluence with the phrenic vessels. The sphenocleidomastoid block has been removed, revealing the phrenic vessels and the adrenal vein in the fibrous tissue.

The surgeon enters the space below the left suprarenal gland and above the diaphragm, lifting the suprarenal gland from the diaphragm. The diaphragmatic vessels are visible as important landmarks, with small branches entering from the left side of the phrenic vessels. The space is fully opened, revealing the diaphragm muscle and facilitating access to the vein below.

The surgeon carefully navigates around the renal pedicle and the renal vein, ensuring not to confuse the large suprarenal gland with adjacent structures. The adrenal vessels are controlled using a 5-millimeter tube, allowing for clear visualization of the adrenal pedicle. The surgical field includes the renal capsule and upper polar artery, with attention to the spatial relationship between the suprarenal gland and kidney.

The surgeon identifies the adrenal vein, which is large and runs perpendicular to the artery. The adrenal vein is ligated, sealed, and cut, allowing the dissection to progress laterally in the direction of the gastro-duodenal region, while keeping the renal vessels in view below.

The surgeon removes the fat surrounding the suprarenal gland without excising the gland itself, ensuring the suprarenal is free from the upper polar of the kidney. The perinephric fat is cut at the level of the kidney capsule, which is clearly identified. Pneumo peritoneum is inserted to facilitate dissection, and blunt dissection is used to separate fat from the kidney capsule, taking care not to damage the kidney or its vasculature.

The surgeon visualizes the upper polar renal vessel, securing it at the apex of the upper pole. The pre-nephritic path is intact, ensuring the adrenal gland remains undisturbed. The plane and capsule of the suprarenal gland are preserved, facilitating the complete excision of the adrenal area between the perineal gland and the upper pole.

The surgeon continues the dissection through the perinephric and retroperitoneal fat surrounding the suprarenal gland, ensuring the adrenal capsule remains intact. The diaphragm is clearly visible, and the adrenal gland is carefully manipulated without direct grasping to prevent capsule rupture. The gland is pushed and lifted using surrounding fat for leverage.

The adrenal gland is completely free from surrounding attachments, and the surgical team prepares to place it into an endobag for protection. The bed of the adrenal gland is visible, and the gland is carefully maneuvered without grasping to ensure it is safely enclosed in the endobag.
